metadata.title: Income and Taxes
metadata.subtitle: Understanding What Happens to Your Paycheck

content.title: Income and Taxes
content.subtitle: Understanding What Happens to Your Paycheck

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Maria earns $48,000 per year ($4,000 monthly) at her first professional job. When she received her first paycheck, she was surprised to see only $2,972 deposited into her account after federal taxes, state taxes, FICA, and benefits deductions.

Why is there such a big difference between what you earn and what you take home?

slides.speakerNotes: Read Maria's paycheck scenario aloud. Ask the discussion question: 'Have you or anyone you know been surprised by how much smaller their paycheck was than expected?' Allow 2-3 students to share experiences. This introduces the fundamental concept of gross vs. net income that students need to understand for realistic budgeting.

objectives.description: Distinguish between gross income (total earnings) and net income (take-home pay) after deductions.
Recognize mandatory deductions (federal taxes, state taxes, FICA) and voluntary deductions (401k, insurance).
Understand the purpose and importance of the W-4 form and how it affects your tax withholding.
Compute take-home pay after various deductions and analyze how this affects financial planning.

terms.definition: The total amount of money earned before any deductions are taken out—your "on paper" salary.
terms.example: You earn $4,000/month salary. This is your gross income before taxes and deductions.

terms.definition: The amount you actually receive after all deductions—your "take-home pay" that hits your bank account.
terms.example: After taxes and deductions, you receive $3,100/month. This is your net income.

slides.speakerNotes: Review all 5 key terms (this slide shows 2; next slide shows 3 more). Emphasize the gross vs. net distinction—this is THE foundational concept students must understand. Create two columns on the board: Mandatory deductions (legally required) vs. Voluntary deductions (employee choice). Quick check: Have students sort common deductions into the correct categories (e.g., federal income tax = mandatory, 401(k) = voluntary, FICA = mandatory, health insurance = voluntary).

terms.definition: Federal Insurance Contributions Act taxes that fund Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%).
terms.example: 7.65% of your gross income goes to FICA—you'll benefit from these programs in retirement.

terms.definition: A tax form you complete when starting a job that tells your employer how much federal tax to withhold.
terms.example: Wrong settings = owing taxes or getting smaller paychecks. Update it when life changes!

content.headerTitle: From Gross to Net: Your Paycheck Journey

leftPanel.title: The 20-30% Gap
paragraphs: The difference between what you earn and what you receive is often 20-30% of your gross income or more.
These deductions aren't just "lost money"—they fund government programs, personal benefits, and your future security.
Understanding this gap is essential for creating realistic budgets based on what you'll actually receive.

highlightBox.text: In {{STATE_NAME}}, state income tax is {{INCOME_TAX_RATE}}%, which affects your take-home pay.

infoCard.title: Key Insight:
items: State tax varies by where you live
Budget based on NET, not gross!

leftPanel.title: Why Your W-4 Matters
paragraphs: When you start a new job, the W-4 form determines how much federal tax your employer withholds from each paycheck.
Too little withholding: You might owe taxes and penalties when you file your return.
Too much withholding: Smaller paychecks all year, but a larger refund—essentially an interest-free loan to the government.

highlightBox.text: Update your W-4 when: Getting married/divorced, having children, starting a second job, or experiencing major income changes.

scenario.name: Alex vs. Jamie: Same Salary, Different Choices
paragraphs: Both start jobs at age 22 with $40,000 annual salaries. Alex immediately contributes 6% to a 401(k) with a 3% employer match, reducing take-home pay slightly.
Jamie takes the full paycheck now, planning to "start saving later." At age 30, Jamie finally starts contributing the same amount.
The 8-year head start makes an enormous difference: By retirement at 67, Alex has $1.2 million while Jamie has only $640,000—a difference of over half a million dollars.

layoutData.title: Benefits Are Part of Your Compensation
paragraphs: Voluntary deductions reduce your paycheck but provide significant value. Understanding these helps you make smart choices about your total compensation package.
bulletPoints: 401(k) Match: Employer matching is "free money"—a 100% return on your contribution!
Health Insurance: Employer-subsidized plans cost less than individual coverage.
Pre-Tax Savings: HSA/FSA contributions reduce taxable income while covering expenses.
Tax Reduction: 401(k) contributions lower your taxable income today.
Life Insurance: Basic coverage often provided free by employers.
Disability Insurance: Protects your income if you can't work.

keyPoint.text: 💰 A job with $50K salary + $10K in benefits is worth more than $55K with no benefits!

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS TO ADDRESS:

1. 'My employer is taking my money' → CLARIFY: Mandatory deductions are required by law and fund programs you'll use (Social Security in retirement, Medicare for healthcare after 65). Voluntary deductions are YOUR choice and often provide valuable benefits like employer 401(k) matching.

2. 'I should skip 401(k) to get more money now' → CLARIFY: Skipping retirement contributions means losing employer matching (literally free money—a 100% return!) and tax-deferred compound growth. The Alex/Jamie example in the lesson shows this costs hundreds of thousands of dollars over time.

3. 'Getting a big tax refund is great' → CLARIFY: A large refund means you over-withheld throughout the year—you gave the government an interest-free loan of YOUR money. Better W-4 adjustments mean more money in each paycheck that you can use or invest throughout the year.

Proactively address these during the lesson, especially #2 which directly contradicts students' short-term thinking.

content.question:
If you saw a job posting for $50,000/year, how much would you expect to actually take home each month in {{STATE_NAME}}?

What factors would affect this amount?

leftPanel.title: Real-World Impact
paragraphs: Budgeting: Create realistic budgets based on net income, not gross.
Tax Planning: Avoid surprises at tax time by understanding withholding.
Benefit Maximization: Take full advantage of employer-offered benefits.
Career Decisions: Compare job offers based on total compensation, not just salary.

highlightBox.text: The financial habits you establish now will impact your well-being for decades.

leftPanel.title: State-Specific Considerations
paragraphs: Living in {{STATE_NAME}} means specific tax rates affect your take-home pay differently than other states.
Some states have no income tax at all (like Texas, Florida), while others (like California) have rates over 13%.
This is an important factor when comparing job offers in different locations or considering relocation.

highlightBox.text: A $60K salary in {{STATE_NAME}} gives you different take-home pay than the same salary in another state!

content.question:
Would you rather have a larger paycheck now or maximize your retirement contributions early?

What factors influence your decision?

layoutData.title: Don't Make These Errors
paragraphs: Many young workers make costly mistakes when it comes to understanding their paychecks and taxes. Here's what to avoid:
bulletPoints: Budgeting on Gross: Plan your spending based on net income, not the salary number.
Ignoring 401(k) Match: Not contributing enough to get full employer match = leaving free money.
Wrong W-4 Settings: Leads to owing taxes or giving the government an interest-free loan.
Skipping Benefits Enrollment: Missing open enrollment means waiting a full year for coverage.
Not Reviewing Pay Stubs: Errors happen—check your deductions are correct each pay period.
Delaying Retirement Savings: Every year you wait costs tens of thousands in future wealth.

questions.question: What is the difference between gross income and net income? Why does this matter for budgeting?
Name three mandatory deductions and two voluntary deductions that might appear on a paycheck.
What happens if your W-4 is set to withhold too little federal tax? Too much?
Why is starting retirement contributions early so valuable? What's the role of compound growth?

slides.speakerNotes:
ANSWER KEY:

Q1: Gross income is the total amount earned before deductions; net income is what you actually take home after all deductions. This matters for budgeting because if you plan spending based on gross income, you'll overspend—you don't have access to that money. Always budget using net income.

Q2: Mandatory deductions: (1) Federal income tax, (2) Social Security (FICA), (3) Medicare, (4) State income tax (in most states). Voluntary deductions: (1) 401(k) retirement contributions, (2) Health insurance premiums, (3) Life insurance, (4) HSA/FSA contributions. Accept any correct examples.

Q3: Too little withheld = You'll owe money when filing taxes (plus potential penalties if significantly under-withheld). Too much withheld = Large refund, which means you gave the government an interest-free loan of your money all year instead of having it available in each paycheck.

Q4: Early retirement contributions benefit from compound growth—your money earns returns, then those returns earn returns, creating exponential growth over time. Time is the most powerful factor: The Alex/Jamie example shows starting 8 years earlier nearly doubled retirement savings despite contributing the same amounts. Starting at age 22 vs. 30 can mean hundreds of thousands of dollars difference by retirement.

takeaways.title: Gross ≠ Net
takeaways.description: Your take-home pay is 20-30% less than your salary—always budget based on net income

takeaways.title: Deductions Have Purpose
takeaways.description: Mandatory taxes fund programs; voluntary deductions provide valuable benefits and savings

takeaways.title: W-4 = Control
takeaways.description: Properly completing your W-4 balances immediate cash flow with annual tax obligations

takeaways.title: Start Early
takeaways.description: Early retirement contributions can add hundreds of thousands to your wealth through compounding

main.title: Complete the W-4 Form Activity
main.description: Scenario: Jordan Rivers is a high school student starting a part-time job at a coffee shop—15 hours/week at $15/hour. Jordan is single, has no dependents, and this is Jordan's only job. Help Jordan complete the W-4 form correctly! Consider: How will Jordan's choices affect each paycheck and tax situation at the end of the year?

steps: Complete each section of the form
Explain your choices for each section
Predict how choices affect take-home pay

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
